title: Packages & Access Modifiers
learningObjectives: Recite the visibility matrix for private, default, protected, and public members
Declare packages, choose imports versus fully qualified names, and avoid wildcard pitfalls
Sketch feature-oriented package trees that match realistic service boundaries
Implement encapsulated types with private fields and validated mutators
Relate JPMS exports to everyday public API decisions in modular codebases
Explain packaging mistakes that show up in build failures and on-call incidents

sections.title: Why packages and access control shape every Java codebase
sections.content:
Packages are how Java namespaces scale: they turn a flat sea of class names into a hierarchy that mirrors ownership, deployment units, and mental models. When teams collide on `User` or `Order`, the fix is not clever prefixes in every identifier—it is `com.company.billing.invoice` style roots that make fully qualified names predictable in logs, stack traces, and dependency graphs. Interviewers care because messy packages predict cyclic builds and leaky APIs that haunt every release train. The same discipline shows up when you split a monolith: cohesive directories become service boundaries, so naming choices you make in Phase 1 are early architecture sketches rather than throwaway folders.

Access modifiers are the compiler-enforced contract between those namespaces. `private` keeps representation details inside a class; package-private (default) hides implementation helpers from other jars while keeping collaborators in the same module friendly; `protected` exposes a deliberate extension surface to subclasses even across package lines; `public` is your published API that other teams compile against. Misusing them shows up as “works on my machine” builds that fail in CI when someone moved a class to another package, or as subtle `IllegalAccessError` surprises when class loaders disagree. Reviewers listen for whether you can articulate who may call a method without opening the IDE, because that skill prevents accidental semver breaks when a helper class graduates to shared infrastructure.

Imports are not runtime magic—they are compile-time shortcuts so you can write `List` instead of `java.util.List`. Wildcards reduce typing but blur review diffs; static imports of constants can clarify intent or harm readability depending on discipline. Production codebases favour explicit imports in shared modules so merges stay legible, IDEs refactor safely, and accidental name clashes surface early instead of during a customer deploy. Teaching juniors to read the import section is teaching them to see hidden coupling: a file that suddenly imports another feature’s internals is often the first sign of a layering violation that will cost a sprint later.

Day eight’s assignment forces you to memorise the visibility matrix, justify private fields with validation, sketch a coherent package tree for an e-commerce slice, and implement a `Person` type whose setters reject nonsense. That sequence mirrors how you defend a pull request: name the rule, show the table, then prove the code enforces it with messages an interviewer can read without scrolling. When you can connect the matrix to a concrete `IllegalArgumentException`, you sound like someone who has shipped code, not someone who memorised a textbook table for a morning quiz.

During on-call, package and access issues appear as `IllegalAccessError` after class-loader tricks, accidental `public` DTO fields mutated by deserialisers, or “helpful” `protected` hooks that became extension traps for distant teams. Debugging means tracing which jar version is on the classpath, whether JPMS exports hide a type you thought was public, and whether reflective frameworks bypassed your accessors. `protected` is the most misunderstood modifier—subclasses in other packages can access it, but arbitrary classes in those packages cannot, and default access never substitutes for that rule. Incidents that look like mystery nulls are sometimes objects constructed through reflection that skipped your package-private guardrails, which is why pairing visibility with validation at boundaries remains a recurring on-call theme.

This day trains you to speak like a module owner: start with the public surface, list what stays package-private, explain why `protected` is or is not justified, and end with one test that would fail if a field were made public. Security-sensitive services rely on package-private constructors plus static factories so collaborators in the same package construct validated instances while external callers see only interfaces. On-call you will thank disciplined packages when stack traces show `com.payments.gateway` immediately, log correlation IDs attach to the right bounded context, and feature toggles target packages instead of stringly-typed class names. When you mentor someone through their first production bug, ask which package owned the failing type and whether visibility matched the story told in design review—that question reframes the incident as a teachable architecture moment instead of a personal mistake.

sections.title: Theory and Internals — namespaces, imports, and the visibility matrix
sections.content:
### Plain-language overview

A **package** is a namespace folder path tied to the `package` declaration at the top of a compilation unit. It groups related types, controls visibility, and mirrors how artefacts ship in JARs and modules. **Access modifiers** (`private`, package-private, `protected`, `public`) decide who may reference fields, methods, and constructors at compile time. **Interview angle:** Explain why `public` on a DTO field is a semver hazard.

### 1. Declaring packages and directory layout

Source roots contain folders matching the package: `src/main/java/com/acme/billing/Invoice.java` begins with `package com.acme.billing;`. The JVM loads classes by binary name `com.acme.billing.Invoice`. **Interview angle:** Mismatch between folder path and declaration is a compile error.

### 2. Fully qualified names versus imports

You may always use the FQN `java.util.List` in code; `import java.util.List;` lets you write `List`. Imports never affect runtime behaviour. **Interview angle:** Wildcard `import java.util.*` imports types only, not subpackages.

### 3. Package-private (default) access

No modifier means visible to types in the **same package** only—not “friendly to subclasses” unless they also sit in that package. **Interview angle:** Moving a class to another package breaks callers that relied on default access.

### 4. `public` and published APIs

`public` types and members are callable from any compilation unit that can see the class on the classpath/module path. Libraries should treat `public` as semver-bound. **Interview angle:** Expose interfaces; keep concrete classes package-private when possible.

### 5. `protected` and inheritance

`protected` members are visible to subclasses **and** other classes in the same package. Subclasses in **different packages** still inherit access. **Interview angle:** Contrast with default—subclass across packages cannot see package-private members.

### 6. `private` and encapsulation

`private` limits visibility to the **top-level class** that declares the member; nested classes have special access rules to outer privates. **Interview angle:** Private fields plus validated setters preserve invariants under refactors.

### 7. Visibility matrix (memorise)

| Modifier | Same class | Same package | Subclass (other pkg) | Unrelated other pkg |
|----------|------------|--------------|----------------------|---------------------|
| private | yes | no | no | no |
| default | yes | yes | no | no |
| protected | yes | yes | yes | no |
| public | yes | yes | yes | yes |

**Interview angle:** Recite the table without hedging on `protected`.

### 8. Single public type per file convention

A public top-level class must live in a file named `ClassName.java`. Additional package-private top-level classes may share the file. **Interview angle:** Useful for hiding small helpers next to a façade.

### 9. Classpath, modules, and visibility

Java Platform Module System adds `exports` and `opens` constraints beyond language visibility. **Interview angle:** Strong encapsulation can hide even `public` types from other modules.

### 10. Static imports

`import static java.lang.Math.PI;` pulls static members into scope; overuse harms readability. **Interview angle:** Reserve for well-known constants in narrow scopes.

### 11. Naming and reverse DNS

`com.company.product.feature` avoids global clashes and encodes ownership. **Interview angle:** Packages are organisational, not security boundaries alone.

### 12. Layered versus feature packages

Layered: `...web`, `...service`, `...repository`. Feature: `...order`, `...payment` each holding local types. **Interview angle:** Feature packages ease microservice extraction.

### 13. Encapsulation and getters/setters

Public fields skip validation; accessors centralise rules and logging. **Interview angle:** Tie to assignment `Person` email and age checks.

### 14. Package cycles

Mutual imports across packages signal design strain; break cycles with interfaces or dependency inversion. **Interview angle:** Build tools can detect cycles in graphs.

### 15. Testing and package-private

Tests in the same package (or `module-info` test opens) can exercise package-private APIs intentionally. **Interview angle:** Contrast with production callers that must not see internals.

### 16. Assignment tie-in

Day 8 covers the visibility table, rationale for private fields, e-commerce package sketch, and validated `Person`. **Interview angle:** Map each assignment question to a modifier or packaging rule.

### 17. 60-second interview story

Packages namespace types; default hides within the package; protected extends to subclasses everywhere; public is your contract. Imports are compile-time sugar. Prefer small public surfaces and validate through methods. Draw a tree before coding to avoid cycles.

### 18. Satyverse drill

Write FQN for `ArrayList`. List who can read a `protected` field from a subclass in another package. Name one type that should stay package-private in a library.

### 19. Pitfall rehearsal table

| Symptom | Likely cause | Fix |
|---------|--------------|-----|
| cannot access | wrong package vs modifier | widen or move type |
| cyclic compile | mutual package deps | extract interface |
| semver break | public class removed | deprecate first |

### 20. Interview angle — closing

Close with **tests** proving illegal states throw and **package diagrams** showing intentional boundaries.

### 21. Modifier quick map

| Goal | Modifier |
|------|----------|
| Hide field | private |
| Module helper | default |
| Extension hook | protected |
| API | public |

### Packaging discipline layer 1

Modules and packages are the grammar of large Java systems; visibility choices age like APIs because compiled dependents embed names. When protected hooks that become accidental extension surfaces across teams, reviewers demand narrower exports, factories in friend packages, or JPMS qualified exports documented in ADRs. **Interview angle:** Name one type you would keep package-private and one consumer that must never see it.

sections.title: Common Pitfalls
items: Treating `protected` like “subclass only” — symptom is surprise compile errors from same-package callers or missing access from distant subclasses; detect in pairing; fix restate the matrix aloud.
Wildcard imports during refactors — symptom is ambiguous `List` or `Date` clashes; detect in CI compile; fix replace with explicit imports.
Public fields on shared models — symptom is illegal state after JSON binding; detect in schema reviews; fix accessors with validation.
Moving classes without compatibility plan — symptom is `NoClassDefFoundError` in dependents; detect with japicmp; fix deprecate shims or coordinate releases.
Giant `util` packages with cycles — symptom is slow builds and tangled reviews; detect with dependency graphs; fix split by feature or introduce interfaces.
Relying on default access across modules — symptom is sudden visibility failures when JPMS enabled; detect module compile; fix export/open or narrow API.
Subclassing for package visibility hacks — symptom is brittle templates; detect in architecture reviews; fix factories in same package or module friends.
Ignoring test placement — symptom is production code made `public` solely for tests; detect in coverage gaps; fix same-package tests or test fixtures module.

sections.title: Exercise — Day 8 assignment: visibility matrix, encapsulation story, package sketch, Person validation
sections.problem:
Implement `arch.day8.Day8Exercise` covering **assignments_phase1.json** Day 8 themes (modifier table recall, why private fields matter, e-commerce package structure, validated `Person`).

Requirements:
1. Print **four** lines labelled `matrix-row:` summarising `public`, `protected`, default, `private` visibility in plain words (same class / package / subclass-other-pkg / world).
2. Print **three** lines labelled `encap:` stating problems with public fields and benefits of private fields plus getters/setters (tie to validation).
3. Print **six** lines labelled `pkg:` showing a coherent feature-based package path list for `Order`, `OrderService`, `OrderRepository`, `Product`, `Customer`, `PaymentGateway` under a shared `com.shop` root (one path per line).
4. Add a static nested `Person` with **private** `String name`, `int age`, `String email`; constructor assigning `name` and calling validated setters for age/email; getters; `setAge` rejecting outside **0–150** with `IllegalArgumentException` message exactly `Invalid age: ` + age; `setEmail` rejecting `null` or missing `@` with `IllegalArgumentException` message starting `Invalid email:`; `toString()` exactly `Person{name='NAME', age=AGE, email='EMAIL'}` pattern matching the assignment.
5. In `main`, build `Person("Alice", 30, "alice@example.com")`, print the person, call `setAge(31)`, print exactly `Updated age: 31`.
6. Add a comment noting that `Person` stays `static` nested only for this single-file exercise—production would usually be its own top-level type in a domain package.
hints: Match exception messages exactly—autograders and reviewers compare strings.
Package paths should read like a real tree, not random tokens.
Constructor can set `name` directly but should route age/email through setters for one validation path.

sections.title: Interview Drill

conceptual.question: What problem do Java packages solve at scale?
conceptual.answer: For **What problem do Java packages solve at scale?** in the context of **Packages & Access Modifiers**, start from JVM internals instead of only syntax. The JVM executes operations through concrete bytecode instructions and memory models, so data representation and control decisions directly affect runtime behavior. In production, weak conceptual understanding becomes incident fuel: hidden allocations, poor branch behavior, or boundary errors increase latency, retry pressure, and GC churn. Diagnose with real tooling: use `jcmd <pid> GC.heap_info` for memory posture, `jstat -gcutil <pid> 1000 10` for GC pressure trends, and `javap -c` to inspect what bytecode is actually emitted for critical logic. Version awareness matters too: Java 8/11/17/21 differ in defaults and runtime optimizations, so migration testing must validate both correctness and performance. Keep the explanation fresher-friendly by pairing internal mechanism with one simple concrete example, but keep staff-level depth by connecting every concept to an operational consequence and an observable metric.

followUps.question: Interview follow-up: how do **package boundaries** affect **production** incidents?
followUps.answer: For namespacing, you see illegal reflective access after upgrades, semver breaks when public types move, and cyclic dependencies that stall releases. You document exported packages, add japicmp or similar checks, and enforce dependency rules in CI. You verify with integration tests that only intended packages appear in stack traces for external callers.

followUps.question: What **trap** might follow your answer?
followUps.answer: Interviewers may ask about JPMS `exports` versus language `public`, or OSGi class loaders. You respond with a clear split: compile-time visibility versus module readability, default deny in modules, and keeping DTO mapping layers explicit. You close with one test that would fail if a package-private helper leaked into a public factory signature.

seniorScenario.question: Library published `public` impl classes teams subclassed—now semver nightmare?
seniorScenario.answer:
**Immediate response:** I stabilize impact first for the Packages & Access Modifiers path by checking user-facing error rate, p95/p99 latency, and rollback options before touching code. I collect runtime evidence with `jcmd <pid> VM.version`, `jcmd <pid> VM.flags`, `jstack <pid>`, and a short `jstat -gcutil <pid> 1000 20` sample so decisions are data-driven within the first five minutes.

**Root cause:** For **Library published `public` impl classes teams subclassed—now semver nightmare?**, the usual root cause is mismatch between intended Java behavior and actual JVM execution characteristics (allocation shape, bytecode branch paths, or boundary assumptions). Under realistic load, this mismatch manifests as exception spikes, throughput collapse, or GC pause amplification that may not appear in narrow local tests.

**Fix:** I apply a narrow correction with explicit guardrails: harden edge conditions, remove avoidable boxing/copying in hot paths, and add deterministic fallback logic where needed. I verify with targeted tests, canary rollout metrics, `javap -c` spot checks for emitted bytecode, and post-deploy SLO comparisons to confirm the change improves behavior without side effects.

**Prevention:** I codify prevention in process and architecture: add checklist rules for this topic in code review, enforce boundary/performance tests in CI, and track dashboards that correlate allocation rate, exception counts, and latency. I also document Java 8/11/17/21 migration notes so future upgrades treat runtime behavior as a first-class validation target, not just compile success.

Moving a class to another package is always binary compatible if the class stays `public`.
Package names are enforced security boundaries without modules or class loaders.
Public fields are preferable to getters because they reduce bytecode size in production.

questions.question: For **Packages & Access Modifiers**, which option is most correct in production-grade Java systems?

options.B: Choose JVM-aware behavior with observability and safe rollout
options.C: Ignore edge cases and rely on retries
options.D: Assume all Java versions behave identically

In production this matters because ignoring runtime implications in Packages & Access Modifiers leads to avoidable incidents and slower MTTR.

options.C: Ignore edge cases and rely on retries
options.D: Assume all Java versions behave identically

questions.explanation: Staff-level reasoning connects language behavior to JVM runtime and operational evidence. B is correct. A is wrong because syntax-only thinking misses production risk; C is wrong because retries can amplify bad logic and latency; D is wrong because version differences can impact defaults and performance. In production this matters because ignoring runtime implications in Packages & Access Modifiers leads to avoidable incidents and slower MTTR.

sections.title: Cheatsheet — packages and access control
sections.content:
| Topic | Rule of thumb | Interview one-liner |
|-------|---------------|---------------------|
| Package | reverse-DNS folders | "Namespaces prevent global name collisions." |
| default | same package | "Not friendly—same package name only." |
| protected | package + subclasses | "Kids elsewhere still see protected." |
| private | class wall | "Validation lives at accessor choke points." |
| public API | semver surface | "Every public type is a promise." |
| import | compile alias | "Zero runtime effect, clarity only." |
| wildcard | one level | "Does not drag subpackages." |
| FQN | always legal | "Skip imports when names clash." |
| JPMS export | readability gate | "public inside module still can be hidden." |
| cycles | layering smell | "Break with interfaces or inversion." |
| tests | same package | "Package-private helpers stay testable." |
| static import | tiny scope | "Great for one constant, noisy for dozens." |
| feature pkg | cohesion | "Move together when service splits." |
| layer pkg | horizontal cut | "Fine until cycles appear." |
| helper class | package-private | "Hide what is not part of the story." |
